import { useTheme } from "next-themes"

/**
 * Compatibility shim: LLMs often generate `import { useTheme } from '@/hooks/use-theme'`.
 * This re-exports next-themes which is the actual theme provider.
 *
 * Preferred usage: import { useTheme } from "next-themes" directly.
 */
export { useTheme }

# Genesis Base Template v2 - AI Development Guide ## Core Rules 1. **Work ONLY in `src/`** - Create/modify files only under `src/`, never touch files outside 2. **Don't modify `src/main.tsx`** - Entry point is fixed 3. **Start with `src/App.tsx`** - Root component of the application 4. **Use `@/` imports** - `import { cn } from '@/lib/utils'` not relative paths ## File Structure ``` src/ ├── App.tsx # Root component (start here) ├── main.tsx # Entry point (don't modify) ├── index.css # Tailwind + CSS variables ├── components/ui/ # Base UI components (Button, Dialog, etc.) ├── components/ai-elements/ # Pre-built AI chat UI components └── lib/ ├── utils.ts # cn() utility └── agent-chat/v2/ # Agent Chat SDK (useChat + createAgentChat) ``` ## Available Dependencies ### Core - react 18.3, react-dom 18.3, typescript 5.4, tailwindcss 3.4 ### UI & Styling - **@radix-ui/react-\*** - Dialog, Dropdown Menu, Select, Switch, Tabs, Tooltip, Progress, Separator - **lucide-react** 0.542 - Icons (1000+ available) - **framer-motion** 12.9 - Animations - **next-themes** 0.4 - Dark mode - **tailwind-merge** + **clsx** - `cn()` utility - **class-variance-authority** 0.7 - Variant styling - **@radix-ui/colors** 3.0 - Color system ### Forms & Validation - **react-hook-form** 7.54 + **@hookform/resolvers** 3.4 - **zod** 3.25 - Schema validation ### State & Routing - **zustand** 4.5 - State management - **react-router-dom** 6.30 - Routing - **react-oidc-context** 3.3 - OIDC auth client - **axios** 1.12 - HTTP client ### UI Components - **cmdk** 1.1 - Command palette - **sonner** 2.0 - Toasts - **@hello-pangea/dnd** 18.0 - Drag & drop - **@formkit/auto-animate** 0.8 - Auto animations - **react-textarea-autosize** 8.5 - Auto-growing textarea - **react-intersection-observer** 9.16 - Visibility detection - **react-error-boundary** 5.0 - Error boundaries ### Content & Data - **react-markdown** 10.1 + **remark-gfm** 4.0 - Markdown - **recharts** 2.15 - Charts - **date-fns** 4.1 - Date utilities ### AI Chat - **@ai-sdk/react** - `useChat` hook for AI chat interfaces - **ai** - AI SDK core (types, transports) - **ai-elements** - Pre-built chat UI components at `@/components/ai-elements/` - **ulidx** - ULID generation for message IDs ## Authentication (OIDC) A pre-built auth wrapper is available at `@/lib/genesis-auth`. Use it instead of configuring `AuthProvider` manually. ### When to add auth Add auth when the app needs to identify individual users (login, signup, per-user data, multi-user features). Do NOT add auth for single-purpose tools with no user concept. ### Usage ```tsx import { GenesisAuth } from '@/lib/genesis-auth'; function App() { return ( ); } ``` Access user profile after login: ```tsx import { useAuth } from 'react-oidc-context'; function Profile() { const auth = useAuth(); if (!auth.isAuthenticated) { return ; } const { email, name, preferred_username, sub } = auth.user?.profile ?? {}; return
Hello {name}
; } ``` - `auth.signinRedirect()` — trigger login (Genesis provides the login/signup UI) - `auth.signoutRedirect()` — logout - `auth.isAuthenticated` — check login state - `auth.user?.profile` — `{ email, name, preferred_username, sub }` Do not build custom login forms or custom auth flows unless explicitly asked. ## Pre-built Components **Base UI** (`@/components/ui/`) — Button, Dialog, Select, Switch, Tabs, etc. **AI Elements** (`@/components/ai-elements/`) — Pre-built chat UI components: - `Conversation`, `ConversationContent`, `ConversationScrollButton` — Scrollable chat container - `Message`, `MessageContent`, `MessageResponse` — Message bubbles with streaming markdown - `PromptInput`, `PromptInputTextarea`, `PromptInputFooter`, `PromptInputSubmit` — Chat input form - `Suggestions`, `Suggestion` — Quick-reply suggestion pills - `Reasoning`, `ReasoningTrigger`, `ReasoningContent` — Collapsible thinking display - `CodeBlock` — Syntax-highlighted code with copy button See `src/lib/agent-chat/v2/README.md` for full Agent Chat SDK docs + AI Elements usage examples. Create custom components freely in `src/components/` using Radix UI primitives. ## CSS Design System Use semantic color classes (automatically supports light/dark mode): - `bg-background text-foreground` - Page default - `bg-primary text-primary-foreground` - Primary buttons - `bg-secondary text-secondary-foreground` - Secondary elements - `bg-muted text-muted-foreground` - Disabled/subtle - `bg-accent text-accent-foreground` - Highlights - `bg-destructive text-destructive-foreground` - Delete/error - `bg-card text-card-foreground` - Cards - `border-border` - Borders - `ring-ring` - Focus rings ## Component Patterns ### TypeScript - Define explicit Props interfaces for all components - Use `React.FC` and function declarations - Use Zod schemas with `z.infer` for form types ### Styling - Use Tailwind utility classes - Use `cn()` from `@/lib/utils` for conditional classes - Use semantic color classes for theme consistency ### File Organization - `src/components/` - Reusable components - `src/pages/` - Route pages - `src/hooks/` - Custom hooks - `src/stores/` - Zustand stores - `src/lib/` - Utilities ## Key Libraries Usage ### Routing - Use `react-router-dom` with `BrowserRouter`, `Routes`, `Route` ### State Management - Use `zustand` with `create()` for global state ### Forms - Use `react-hook-form` with `useForm()` and `zodResolver()` for validation ### Icons - Import from `lucide-react`: `import { Home, User, Settings } from 'lucide-react'` ### Dark Mode - Use `next-themes` with `ThemeProvider` and `useTheme()` hook ### HTTP - Use `axios` for API calls ## Summary React 18 + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS template. Work only in `src/`. Use Radix UI primitives for accessible components, Zustand for state, React Router for routing, react-hook-form + Zod for forms, Lucide for icons. Build custom components as needed.

# Echo Heist — LOW Severity Final Pass (2026-04-08c) **Target file:** `previews/echo-heist/current.html` **Issued by:** Player Clarity Auditor · Studio OS **Prerequisites:** All three prior patches must be applied first: 1. `echo-heist-patch-2026-04-07.md` (11 engine/UX fixes) 2. `echo-heist-patch-2026-04-08.md` (Terminal Intro + cost units) 3. `echo-heist-patch-2026-04-08b.md` (Guards note + vault 15 s flash) **Audit score before this patch:** 5/7 clear · est. cold-start ~72–78% **Audit score after this patch:** 7/7 clear · est. cold-start ~88–92% **Patches in this doc:** 2 (LOW-severity gaps — closes out the audit entirely) Apply both fixes in order. Each section has an exact FIND string + DROP-IN replacement. All changes are purely presentational; no game state or scoring logic is modified. --- ## FIX A — Esc nuance: "why does skipping cost heat?" inline tooltip ### Gap addressed **LOW · Anxiety Point — Esc label explains the cost but not the reason** After patch -08, the hint line reads `Esc = skip (+20 Heat)`. The quantity is now explicit but a curious player who pauses on it will still wonder *why* pressing Escape costs heat at all — the game world logic is never stated. This is low-impact because the cost amount is visible, but it is the last piece of vocabulary left unexplained. ### What this patch adds A compact inline tooltip `` that appears on **hover** over the `Esc = skip (+20 Heat)` segment only. The tooltip reads: > *Leaving a node mid-hack triggers a proximity alert.* This is one sentence, entirely in the game's own fiction, and requires no new UI chrome — it uses a CSS `:hover` title-attribute tooltip for zero JS overhead, plus a visible styled tooltip for browsers that render it poorly. Two sub-steps: 1. Wrap the Esc segment in a `` with a styled hover tooltip inside the hint line. 2. Add one CSS rule for the tooltip appearance. --- ### Step A-1 — Wrap the Esc segment in the hint line HTML After all prior patches the hint line looks like this (with the guard-note span at the end): ```html
Enter to submit · Esc = skip (+20 Heat) · H = hint (−50 score pts) · F = scaffold
``` **FIND:** ```html
Enter to submit · Esc = skip (+20 Heat) · H = hint (−50 score pts) · F = scaffold
``` **REPLACE:** ```html
Enter to submit · Esc = skip (+20 Heat)Leaving a node mid-hack triggers a proximity alert. · H = hint (−50 score pts) · F = scaffold
``` **What changed:** The text `Esc = skip (+20 Heat)` is now wrapped in `.hint-tooltip-wrap` which reveals a child `.hint-tooltip` on hover. Everything else on the line is unchanged. --- ### Step A-2 — Add the tooltip CSS rules Add the following inside the existing `

# Echo Heist — Clarity Patch (2026-04-08) **Target file:** `previews/echo-heist/current.html` **Issued by:** Player Clarity Auditor · Studio OS **Audit score before patch:** 1.5 / 7 dimensions clear **Estimated cold-start rate before patch:** ~35–40% **Patches in this doc:** 2 (HIGH-severity gaps only) Apply both fixes in order. Each section has an exact FIND string + DROP-IN replacement. Both are surgical: no other code moves, no state changes outside the described scope. --- ## FIX 1 — Terminal Intro Overlay ### Gap addressed **HIGH · Anxiety Point #1 — Penalties before objectives** The first thing a player sees when pressing `E` on any terminal or door is a math prompt with penalty warnings (`costs heat`, `−50`) — before they know the game's goal, what they are typing, or what the consequences actually mean. ### What this patch adds A one-time "first use" intro panel that fires the **very first time any math popup opens**. It sits above the normal prompt, fades in on open, and dismisses automatically once the player presses `Enter` for the first time — at which point the game proceeds normally to their first real math question. It will **never appear again** after the player's first `Enter` keystroke (persisted in `localStorage` under the key `echoHeistMathIntroDone`). ### How it works (state + HTML + JS) **Step 1 — Add one global flag** (after the existing `focusTooltipTimer` line): **FIND:** ```js let focusTooltipShown = false; // true once player has seen the focus-mode tip let focusTooltipTimer = 0; ``` **REPLACE:** ```js let focusTooltipShown = false; // true once player has seen the focus-mode tip let focusTooltipTimer = 0; let mathIntroSeen = false; // true after player presses Enter through the intro once let mathIntroDismissed = false; // true while intro is showing and player hasn't pressed Enter yet try { mathIntroSeen = localStorage.getItem('echoHeistMathIntroDone') === '1'; } catch(e) {} ``` --- **Step 2 — Add the intro HTML panel** (inside `#math-popup`, directly after `

`): **FIND:** ```html

Terminal

``` **REPLACE:** ```html

Terminal

🎯 What you're doing
You're hacking a security node. Solve the math problem to crack it and move deeper into the heist.
🔢 How to answer
Type your answer below and press Enter to submit.
Accepted formats: whole numbers (12) · decimals (3.5) · fractions (3/4) · percents (25%)
⚠️ Heat & penalties
HEAT = how suspicious the guards are (0–100). At 100 you're caught.
Wrong answer → +5 Heat  ·  Skip (Esc) → +20 Heat  ·  Hint (H) → −50 score pts
Press Enter to begin your first challenge ↓
``` --- **Step 3 — Add one CSS rule** (inside the existing `

# Snack Line Shuffle — Gate 6 Teacher Review
**Read time: under 3 minutes · Please keep this copy after signing**
Version 1.0 · 2026-04-08

---

## What is this game?

**Snack Line Shuffle** is a short tablet game for Kindergarten and Grade 1 students. Kids look at a group of cartoon children waiting in a lunch line. Each cartoon child is holding a food tray showing a simple addition problem — things like **2 + 1** or **3 + 0**. The student's job is to sort the line so that the child with the biggest total goes first to the serving window, and the child with the smallest total goes last.

That's it. No reading required. No typing. Just drag, place, and serve.

The game is designed to be played in short bursts — about 5 to 8 minutes — either at a learning station, on a shared classroom tablet, or one-on-one with a teacher.

---

## What skill does it build?

The game builds **addition fluency with sums up to 5**, targeting Kindergarten and early Grade 1. Students are not just computing answers — they are comparing multiple sums at the same time, which requires holding numbers in mind and reasoning about size. This is harder than it sounds for a 5 or 6-year-old, and the game is specifically designed to make that challenge feel fun and fair.

---

## What the game sounds like — 3 sample voice lines

The game uses a friendly narrator voice throughout. Below are three examples of the **amber** lines — the gentle hints the game plays when a student has placed a child in the wrong spot. These are the moments most likely to be overheard in your classroom.

> **"Hmm, let's take another look — who's got more food on their tray?"**

> **"Almost! Remember, the biggest lunch goes closest to the window."**

> **"Let's try swapping those two — which one adds up to more?"**

These lines play softly and only once per mistake. The game never says a student is wrong. It redirects with a question and waits.

---

## What the ordering-direction indicator looks like

In the top corner of the screen, there is a small icon showing a **line of cartoon kids walking toward a serving window**, with a star or glow on the child at the front. This is always visible. It reminds the student which direction "first" means — toward the window — without any words. If a student forgets the goal mid-game, they can look at this icon at any time.

The indicator does not change between rounds. It is the same image, in the same corner, for the entire session.

---

## Two questions for your sign-off

Please read each question and circle **YES** or **NO** based on what you just read above.

 

**1. Based on this description, do you feel this game is appropriate for use in a Kindergarten or Grade 1 classroom setting?**

    **YES**      **NO**

If NO, please note your concern here: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

 

**2. The three voice lines above represent the type of language students will hear during the game. Are you comfortable with this tone and wording in your classroom?**

    **YES**      **NO**

If NO, please note your concern here: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

---

## Signature

By signing below, you confirm that you have read this one-page summary and answered both questions above.

 

Name: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

School: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Date: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Signature: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# Snack Line Shuffle — P1 Playtest Observer Protocol
**Field Guide · One Session · One Observer · One Child**
Version 1.0 · 2026-04-08 · K–1 Grade Band

> **Print this page. Bring a pencil. Do not read aloud any text marked INTERNAL.**
> All child-facing language is in **bold**. Everything else is for your eyes only.

---

## Before You Start — 2-Minute Pre-Session Checklist

| # | Check | ✓ |
|---|-------|---|
| 1 | Device volume at 60–70 % (voice-over audible; not startling) | ☐ |
| 2 | Game is on the title screen, not mid-session | ☐ |
| 3 | Pencil and tally sheet ready | ☐ |
| 4 | Timer app open (you will need it for Gate 7 and Gate 10) | ☐ |
| 5 | No adult other than you visible or audible to the child | ☐ |
| 6 | Child is settled; confirm they are not hungry, tired, or distressed | ☐ |

If any check cannot be satisfied, **postpone the session**. Do not proceed with a compromised environment.

---

## SECTION 1 — Gate 7: Rule Comprehension Question Script

**Gate number:** 7 of 14
**Purpose:** Confirm the child understands the ordering rule *before* production build is locked.
**When to administer:** Immediately after the child completes Round 2 (the first 3-kid round) without adult help.
**Time limit:** Allow up to 60 seconds for each question. Start timer when you finish speaking.

---

### 1.1 Set-Up Line (Say This Verbatim)

**"Great job! I want to ask you two quick questions about the game. There are no wrong answers — I just want to hear what you think."**

Pause. Wait for child to look at you or nod.

---

### 1.2 Question 1 — Ordering Direction

Point to the line of kids on screen (or to the serving counter end of the line).

**"Which kid gets to the window first — the one with the biggest snack total, or the one with the smallest snack total?"**

| Child says | Code | Action |
|---|---|---|
| "Biggest" / "most" / "the most food" / points to highest-total kid | `G7-DIR-PASS` | Continue to Q2 |
| "Smallest" / "least" / points to lowest-total kid | `G7-DIR-FAIL` | Note, continue to Q2 anyway |
| Repeats the rule correctly but inverted ("smallest goes first") | `G7-DIR-FAIL` | Note, continue to Q2 |
| "I don't know" / no response after 60 s | `G7-DIR-NORESP` | Note, continue to Q2 |
| Describes position (e.g., "the one at the front") without magnitude language | `G7-DIR-PARTIAL` | Ask once: **"What makes that kid be at the front?"** Code the follow-up answer |

---

### 1.3 Question 2 — Computation Requirement

Show (or describe) a new hypothetical pair on paper: write **3+0** and **2+2** side by side.

**"If one kid has 3+0 on their tray, and another kid has 2+2, which one goes closer to the window?"**

Do not say the totals aloud. Do not point to either expression as "bigger."

| Child response | Code | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Says "2+2" (correctly identifies the higher total) AND gives any computation evidence (counting, finger use, verbal adding) | `G7-COMP-PASS` | Ideal |
| Says "2+2" with NO visible computation evidence | `G7-COMP-INFER` | Possible but unclear; flag for coding rubric review |
| Says "3+0" (picks larger first addend — M1 error) | `G7-COMP-M1FAIL` | **Critical finding.** Note verbatim what child said. |
| Says "3+0" because "3 is bigger than 2" | `G7-COMP-M1VERBAL` | M1 misconception stated explicitly. Record exact words. |
| Cannot compare / "I don't know" after 60 s | `G7-COMP-NORESP` | Note |

**After Q2 — Closing Line (Say This Verbatim):**

**"Thank you! Ready to keep playing?"**

Do not correct wrong answers. Do not explain the answer. Record codes and proceed.

---

## SECTION 2 — Computation Evidence Coding Rubric

**Purpose:** Determine whether observed correct answers reflect genuine computation or a non-math shortcut.
**Use this rubric during any round, not only at Gate 7.**

A correct placement is only coded as **computation-valid** if at least one of the following observable signals is present within 8 seconds of the child picking up a kid to place them.

---

### 2.1 Tier A — Strong Evidence (code `CE-A`)

Any of these signals alone is sufficient:

| Signal | What it looks like |
|---|---|
| **Finger counting** | Child extends fingers while looking at the expression, or touches the screen in a counting pattern |
| **Sub-vocal counting** | Lips moving, barely audible number sequence ("one, two, three…") |
| **Overt verbal addition** | Child says the computation aloud, even partially ("two plus two is four") |
| **Hesitation + gaze shift** | Child pauses (≥2 s) at the expression, eyes move between the two addends, then places |
| **Self-correction** | Child picks up a kid, starts to place, then moves to a different slot — consistent with recomputing |

---

### 2.2 Tier B — Weak Evidence (code `CE-B`)

Present but not conclusive. Flag for pattern review across the session.

| Signal | What it looks like |
|---|---|
| **Expression re-read** | Child's gaze returns to the tray expression after initially looking away |
| **Comparison pause** | Child holds two kids simultaneously and looks between their trays before placing either |
| **Delayed placement** | Child takes longer than 4 s on a trap round compared to their own baseline from non-trap rounds |

---

### 2.3 No Evidence (code `CE-0`)

Record `CE-0` when:
- Child places immediately without any observable pause or motor signal
- Child's gaze does not return to the expression after initially reading it
- Placement speed is uniform across trap and non-trap rounds (no slowdown at trap pairs)

> **INTERNAL NOTE:** `CE-0` on a correct answer in an M1 trap round is the most important data point in the session. It may indicate the child has internalised computation (best case) or is using a non-math strategy that happens to be correct this round (risk case). Flag for follow-up interview.

---

### 2.4 Shortcut Pattern Flags (code `SP-X`)

Record these if observed; they override CE codes:

| Code | Description |
|---|---|
| `SP-FIRST` | Child consistently places the kid with the larger *first* addend first, regardless of total. Will produce wrong placements on all M1 trap rounds. |
| `SP-LARGER` | Child consistently places the kid whose expression *looks bigger* (longer number string, larger numeral size on screen) first. Art-dependent. |
| `SP-SECOND` | Child consistently places the kid with the larger *second* addend first. Sometimes correct, sometimes not — will fail on e.g. 3+2 vs 4+1 if second addend is used alone. |
| `SP-RANDOM` | No discernible strategy; placements appear random. Child may not have understood the rule. |

A child who produces `SP-FIRST` on two or more M1 trap rounds has **confirmed M1 misconception active**. Session continues but note this in the stop/proceed decision (Section 4).

---

## SECTION 3 — Gate 10: Voluntary Continuation Script

**Gate number:** 10 of 14
**Purpose:** Measure intrinsic motivation — does the child choose to keep playing without any adult encouragement?
**When to administer:** Immediately after Round 9 (the first 4-kid round, first round in Core Block B) resolves — either correct serve or time-out.
**Critical constraint:** You must appear neutral. No smiling, nodding, or leaning forward during the Gate 10 window.

---

### 3.1 Transition Into the Gate (Say This Verbatim)

Wait for the round-complete animation to finish. Then:

**"We can stop here if you want, or you can keep playing. It's completely up to you."**

Then: set your pencil down, look at your tally sheet (not at the child), and **start a 15-second timer silently**.

Do not add anything. Do not look at the child. Do not react to what they do.

---

### 3.2 Coding the Response

| What happens within 15 s | Code | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Child taps/clicks to start the next round unprompted | `G10-CONTINUE` | **Pass.** Note time to action (e.g. "3 s") |
| Child says "more" / "again" / "can we keep going?" | `G10-CONTINUE-VERBAL` | **Pass.** Note exact words. |
| Child looks at you and waits | `G10-LOOK` | Neutral. Do not react. If still looking at 15 s, go to 3.3 |
| Child says "done" / "stop" / "no more" | `G10-STOP-VERBAL` | End session. Go to Section 4. |
| Child leaves the device, stands up, or turns away | `G10-STOP-PHYSICAL` | End session. Go to Section 4. |
| No action after 15 s | `G10-TIMEOUT` | Go to 3.3 |

---

### 3.3 If G10-LOOK or G10-TIMEOUT — One Permitted Prompt

Say this once only, in a flat tone:

**"Whatever you feel like doing is fine."**

Restart the 15-second timer. Code the response using the same table above.

If the child still does not act after the second 15-second window, code `G10-TIMEOUT-DOUBLE` and end the session. Do not offer a third prompt.

---

### 3.4 After Gate 10 Resolves

If the child continues: let them play Rounds 10–13 uninterrupted. Do not administer any more scripted questions during gameplay.

If the child stops: say **"That was great, thank you so much."** Transition to the post-session debrief (not covered by this protocol — see Playtest Audit template).

---

## SECTION 4 — Session Fail Conditions & Stop/Proceed Decision Tree

Work through this tree **top to bottom**. Stop at the first `STOP` branch. If you reach the bottom without hitting a STOP, proceed.

---

```
START
│
├─ Is the child visibly distressed, crying, or refusing to engage?
│   YES → STOP. Log: WELFARE-DISTRESS. Do not attempt to continue.
│   NO  → continue ↓
│
├─ Has the child produced SP-FIRST on ≥2 M1 trap rounds (R3, R4, R6, R8)?
│   YES → Is G7-COMP-M1VERBAL also present?
│         YES → STOP. Log: CONFIRMED-M1-BLOCK.
│               Finding: misconception is both active and verbally stated.
│               Do not continue — additional rounds will only reinforce it.
│         NO  → PROCEED with caution. Log: M1-PATTERN-DETECTED.
│               Continue session but note in debrief: rule comprehension
│               intervention needed before next session.
│   NO  → continue ↓
│
├─ Has the child produced CE-0 on every M1 trap round completed so far?
│   YES → Are all placements correct despite CE-0?
│         YES → PROCEED. Log: CE-0-ALL-CORRECT. Flag for follow-up interview.
│               This child may have internalised computation — valuable finding.
│         NO  → Are ≥50% of placements on M1 trap rounds incorrect?
│               YES → STOP. Log: STRATEGY-FAILURE-NO-EVIDENCE.
│                     Child is not computing and is getting traps wrong.
│                     Evidence of random or shortcut strategy with no self-correction.
│               NO  → PROCEED. Log: CE-0-MIXED. Continue monitoring.
│   NO  → continue ↓
│
├─ Did Gate 7 Q1 produce G7-DIR-FAIL?
│   YES → Did the child self-correct at any point during Rounds 0–2?
│         YES → PROCEED. Log: G7-DIR-FAIL-SELFCORRECT.
│               The in-game feedback loop may be working. Flag for design review.
│         NO  → STOP. Log: RULE-NOT-UNDERSTOOD.
│               Child has not acquired the ordering direction rule.
│               Additional play will not resolve this without instruction.
│   NO  → continue ↓
│
├─ Is the round completion time for 4-kid rounds >3× the child's own baseline
│   for 2-kid rounds (from R0 and R1)?
│   YES → Is the child still engaging (looking at screen, moving kids)?
│         YES → PROCEED. Log: SLOW-PACE-ENGAGED. Do not rush the child.
│         NO  → STOP. Log: SLOW-PACE-DISENGAGED. Child has lost the thread.
│   NO  → continue ↓
│
└─ PROCEED. No session fail conditions met.
   Continue to Gate 10 if not yet administered.
   Continue to end of Round 13 if Gate 10 produced G10-CONTINUE.
```

---

## Quick Reference — All Codes at a Glance

| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `G7-DIR-PASS` | Correctly identified largest-total kid goes first |
| `G7-DIR-FAIL` | Inverted ordering direction stated |
| `G7-DIR-PARTIAL` | Position language without magnitude — prompted |
| `G7-DIR-NORESP` | No response to Q1 within 60 s |
| `G7-COMP-PASS` | Correct + computation evidence on M1 pair |
| `G7-COMP-INFER` | Correct but no visible computation |
| `G7-COMP-M1FAIL` | Incorrect — chose larger first addend |
| `G7-COMP-M1VERBAL` | M1 misconception stated verbally |
| `G7-COMP-NORESP` | No response to Q2 within 60 s |
| `CE-A` | Strong computation evidence (any Tier A signal) |
| `CE-B` | Weak computation evidence (Tier B only) |
| `CE-0` | No computation evidence observed |
| `SP-FIRST` | First-addend-size shortcut (M1 pattern) |
| `SP-LARGER` | Visual-size shortcut (art-dependent) |
| `SP-SECOND` | Second-addend-only shortcut |
| `SP-RANDOM` | No discernible strategy |
| `G10-CONTINUE` | Voluntary continuation (action) |
| `G10-CONTINUE-VERBAL` | Voluntary continuation (verbal) |
| `G10-LOOK` | Looked to adult — awaiting prompt |
| `G10-STOP-VERBAL` | Verbal stop request |
| `G10-STOP-PHYSICAL` | Physical disengagement |
| `G10-TIMEOUT` | No response in first 15 s window |
| `G10-TIMEOUT-DOUBLE` | No response in second 15 s window |
| `WELFARE-DISTRESS` | Session stopped — child distress |
| `CONFIRMED-M1-BLOCK` | Session stopped — M1 verbal + pattern |
| `M1-PATTERN-DETECTED` | M1 pattern observed, session continues with flag |
| `RULE-NOT-UNDERSTOOD` | Session stopped — ordering rule not acquired |
| `STRATEGY-FAILURE-NO-EVIDENCE` | Session stopped — no computation + wrong traps |
| `SLOW-PACE-DISENGAGED` | Session stopped — pace issue + disengagement |
| `CE-0-ALL-CORRECT` | No evidence but all correct — flag for interview |
| `CE-0-MIXED` | No evidence, mixed correctness — continue |

---

## What to Do After the Session

1. **Circle** every STOP code reached (if any) in the decision tree.
2. **Transfer** all codes to the Playtest Audit project in Taskade (project `cyt3zvpjf32D1Ddt` or Playtest Audit tracker).
3. **Flag** any session containing `G7-COMP-M1VERBAL` or `CONFIRMED-M1-BLOCK` as a **Priority Analysis** entry — these feed directly into the Misconception Library.
4. **Do not discuss** findings with the child's teacher before the design team has reviewed the session log.

---

*Protocol authored for Snack Line Shuffle P1 build · Studio OS · 2026-04-08*
*Next review: after first 5 sessions completed or at Gate 10 aggregate review, whichever comes first.*
